Land Rover Defender D240 Engine Codes

mainEntity.name: How much does a Land Rover Defender D240 engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned Defender D240 (204DTD) engine typically costs £3,500–£5,800 supply only. Fitting adds £650–£1,300 at independent specialist rates. Use our calculator for a figure specific to your vehicle.

mainEntity.name: Is the Land Rover Defender D240 engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The 204DTD in the D240 is the single-turbo Ingenium diesel, a 2020–2021 short-run engine. It shares the pre-2019 timing chain tensioner risk profile that has defined the Ingenium D's reputation. Post-2019 revisions reduced failure rates but did not eliminate the risk entirely — buy on chain history.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Defender D240?

acceptedAnswer.text: Timing chain tensioner wear (the defining failure mode), oil dilution from DPF regeneration (urban-use risk), and EGR cooler failure (coolant leaks into the exhaust system). All three are well-documented across the 204DTD family.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Defender D240 engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Against a well-kept £25,000 Defender D240, a £4,500 recon is 18% of the car's value — worth it. Against a higher-mileage example, the Land Rover scrap threshold of 60% means replacement remains viable across most D240 values, given the L663's strong residual values.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Defender D240 engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: The engine code 204DTD is listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. The D240 badge was only available 2020–2021 in the L663 Defender, so if your vehicle is a 2020 or early 2021 L663 diesel, it's almost certainly a 204DTD. Tell us your registration and we'll confirm for free.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the 204DTD timing chain, or does it need replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — if caught early. A preventative chain replacement (£1,200–£2,500) is far cheaper than a catastrophic chain snap (£3,500–£5,800 engine replacement). At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately.

problems.title: Timing Chain Tensioner Failure (Pre-2019 Ingenium D)
problems.symptoms: Cold-start rattle, engine warning light, loss of power, metallic noises from the front of the engine.

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: The Ingenium D timing chain tensioner is the single biggest reliability concern on the 204DTD. While the L663 Defender's 2020–2021 production run benefited from post-2019 revisions, this risk is not eliminated — it is merely reduced. Any cold-start rattle from a D240 must be investigated immediately. A £1,200–£2,500 preventative chain replacement is dramatically cheaper than a £3,500+ engine replacement.

problems.title: Oil Dilution From DPF Regeneration
problems.symptoms: Rising oil level on dipstick, diesel smell in oil, DPF warning light, reduced fuel economy.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if oil level is rising

problems.recommendation: The 204DTD's DPF regeneration cycle can push unburnt fuel past the piston rings into the sump, diluting the engine oil and reducing its lubricating properties. This is a particular risk on vehicles used primarily for short urban journeys. Regular oil changes (6,000-mile intervals rather than JLR's extended schedule) and occasional motorway runs are the most effective prevention. If the oil level is rising on the dipstick, book an oil change and DPF inspection immediately.

problems.title: EGR Cooler Failure
problems.symptoms: Coolant loss with no visible external leak, white exhaust smoke, occasional burning smell, engine warning light.

urgency.label: Check
urgency.text: book inspection urgently if coolant is disappearing

problems.recommendation: EGR cooler failure is a known issue across the Ingenium diesel family, including the 204DTD in the L663 Defender. Coolant leaks into the exhaust system can lead to overheating and, if ignored, cylinder head damage. The EGR cooler itself is a replaceable component (£500–£1,200), and there have been JLR service actions covering certain VIN ranges. Check your vehicle's service history for any recall or goodwill work.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: Defender D240 — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Preventative — catches the fault before it becomes catastrophic
Routine maintenance; oil change + forced regen
Straightforward fix if caught before overheating
Piston-valve contact means little is left to repair
Viable on individual injectors; all-four failure often prompts replacement evaluation

buyingChecks.title: Buying This Defender D240? Check These First
buyIf: * Documented timing chain history (proof of tensioner inspection or replacement)
* Confirmed oil change intervals at 6,000 miles or less (not JLR's extended schedule)
* Full service history with specialist attention, not just main dealer stamps
* Urban-use history with regular motorway runs to clear the DPF
avoidIf: * Cold-start rattle with no chain history — this is the defining failure mode
* No proof of regular oil changes (oil dilution risk)
* Service history gaps or long intervals (12,000+ miles between oil changes)
* Engine warning light with no diagnosis — the 204DTD can hide expensive faults

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The Defender D240's 204DTD is a capable, torquey engine that suits the L663 platform well — but it belongs to the pre-2019 Ingenium family that earned a reputation for timing chain tensioner failures. Post-2019 revisions reduced but did not eliminate the risk. This badge's 2020–2021 production window means every D240 is affected by the same design era; buy on chain history, not on low mileage alone.

cta.label: → Compare Defender D240 engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Land Rover Defender D240 (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattle lasting 1–30 seconds (timing chain warning, 204DTD)
* Service History — Including oil change intervals (6,000 miles recommended, not 12,000+)
* Smoke Check — White smoke on startup can indicate EGR coolant leak
* Turbo/Boost Check — Test acceleration; any lag, hesitation, or whistling?
* DPF Regeneration — Check history; short journeys accelerate wear on all diesel Ingenium units
* Oil Consumption — Does it use more than 1L per 1,000 miles? Rising oil level is equally concerning
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved issues?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures (DPF, EGR, NOx sensors)

engineCodes.matchNote: This table's rows exactly match Section 1B's Era Map — the same engine code, years, and generation coverage.
